skill.name: mcp-integration
skill.description: Guides Claude Code plugin developers through integrating Model Context Protocol servers for external tools, services, authentication, and tool usage.
skill.summary: Integrate MCP servers into Claude Code plugins with configuration, authentication, testing, and security guidance.
skill.author: anthropics

security_audit.summary: This official Anthropic skill contains documentation and examples for MCP integration in Claude Code plugins. Static findings reflect expected network URLs, environment variables, and command examples for MCP configuration, not malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Integrate MCP Servers in Claude Code Plugins
content.value_statement: Plugin developers need a reliable way to connect Claude Code to external tools and services. This skill explains MCP server setup, authentication, tool permissions, testing, and operational patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to configure MCP servers with .mcp.json or plugin.json.
Compares stdio, SSE, HTTP, and WebSocket MCP server types.
Shows authentication patterns for OAuth, bearer tokens, API keys, and environment variables.
Describes MCP tool naming and allowed-tools configuration for commands and agents.
Provides guidance for lifecycle management, debugging, testing, and performance.
Documents security practices for HTTPS, token handling, and permission scoping.
limitations: It does not implement a production MCP server for you.
It assumes a Claude Code plugin project and an MCP-compatible service or server.
External service setup still depends on each provider documentation and credentials.
Examples must be adapted before use in security-sensitive environments.

use_cases.title: Add Service Tools to a Plugin
use_cases.description: Configure a plugin so Claude Code can use tools from a hosted or local MCP server.

use_cases.title: Plan Authentication for MCP Access
use_cases.description: Choose OAuth, token headers, or environment variables for a service integration.

use_cases.title: Review MCP Tool Permissions
use_cases.description: Define specific MCP tools that commands and agents can use during workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an MCP Server Type
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a first MCP integration.
prompt_templates.prompt: I am building a Claude Code plugin for [service]. Help me choose between stdio, SSE, HTTP, and WebSocket MCP servers.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Plugin MCP Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an MCP integration plan for my plugin. The service is [service], authentication is [method], and users need these tools: [tools].

prompt_templates.title: Review Authentication Design
prompt_templates.scenario: Team validating token and OAuth handling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my MCP authentication approach for a Claude Code plugin. Check environment variables, token storage assumptions, user setup steps, and permission scope.

prompt_templates.title: Debug MCP Tool Availability
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced troubleshooting for a configured integration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help debug why MCP tools are not available in my Claude Code plugin. Review server type, tool naming, allowed tools, lifecycle, and connection checks.

output_examples.input: A developer asks how to connect a hosted project management service to a Claude Code plugin.
output: Recommendation to use a hosted SSE server when the provider supports OAuth.
Authentication notes that describe first-use browser sign-in and token handling by Claude Code.
A checklist for documenting required service access and allowed MCP tools.

output_examples.input: A team wants a local database MCP server for internal workflows.
output: Guidance to use a stdio MCP server when a local child process is appropriate.
Environment variable requirements for database connection settings and secrets.
Testing steps for startup, tool discovery, error handling, and least-privilege access.

output_examples.input: A plugin command needs permission to call only two MCP tools.
output_examples.output: The skill explains how to name MCP tools and pre-allow specific tool names instead of using broad wildcards.

best_practices: Use secure HTTPS or WSS endpoints for remote MCP servers.
Store credentials in environment variables or provider OAuth flows, not plugin files.
Allow only the specific MCP tools that each command or agent needs.
anti_patterns: Hardcoding tokens, API keys, or database credentials in plugin configuration.
Using wildcard MCP tool permissions when a workflow needs only a few tools.
Shipping examples without documenting required environment variables and setup steps.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you connect Claude Code plugins to MCP servers that expose external tools and services.

faq.question: Which MCP server types does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers stdio, SSE, HTTP, and WebSocket server types with guidance for when to use each one.

faq.question: Does it handle authentication guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers OAuth, bearer tokens, API keys, custom headers, environment variables, and token handling practices.

faq.question: Can it help with MCP tools in commands and agents?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains tool naming, allowed-tools configuration, and common command and agent usage patterns.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends secure transport, scoped permissions, safe credential storage, and careful wildcard use.

faq.question: Does this skill provide a ready-made MCP server?
faq.answer: No. It provides integration guidance and examples, but you must provide or select the MCP server.
